title: Time Complexity of Collection Operations

glossary.definition: A way to describe how the runtime of an operation increases as the amount of data grows.
When two different keys accidentally get mapped to the exact same location in a hash table.

overview.what:
Every Python collection operation has a time complexity — a measure of how the number of steps grows as the collection grows. Big-O notation describes that growth rate:

  O(1) — constant time, the same speed regardless of size (like opening a book to a page number you already know).
  O(n) — linear time, you must scan every element in the worst case.
  O(n log n) — log-linear time, typical of efficient sorting algorithms.

Think of a dict or set as a filing cabinet with labelled drawers: you walk straight to the right drawer in O(1). A list used for membership testing is an unlabelled pile: you must rifle through every item, O(n).

Here are the key operations and their average-case complexities:

  list: index access `xs[i]` O(1), append O(1) amortised, insert/delete at position 0 O(n), `x in xs` O(n), sort O(n log n).

  dict: get/set/delete by key O(1), `key in d` O(1).

  set: add/discard/`x in s` O(1).

  tuple: index access `t[i]` O(1), `x in t` O(n) (tuples have no hash table — they are scanned linearly, just like lists).

The practical takeaway: whenever you need to check whether a value exists in a collection, convert it to a set or use a dict. A membership test against a list of 1 000 000 items is up to 1 000 000× slower than the same test against a set.

Example — filtering allowed user IDs from an API request:

  allowed = {'u-101', 'u-202', 'u-303'}        # set, O(1) lookup
  payload_ids = ['u-202', 'u-999', 'u-101']
  valid = [uid for uid in payload_ids if uid in allowed]

Example — building a fast cache index:

  cache = {}                                     # dict, O(1) lookup
  for record in db_rows:
      cache[record['id']] = record
overview.why: Choosing the wrong collection for the job is the most common performance mistake in Python. A single `if item in big_list` inside a loop can silently turn an O(n) endpoint into an O(n²) endpoint. Understanding these costs lets you pick the right structure before profiling tells you something is slow.

why_learning_this: Explains why swapping a list for a set can turn a slow API endpoint from seconds to milliseconds
Gives you a mental checklist when choosing between list, dict, set, and tuple in production code
Commonly tested in interviews: 'Why is dict lookup O(1)?' and 'What is the cost of list.insert(0, x)?'

common_mistakes.title: Using `in` on a list inside a loop
common_mistakes.explanation: `x in my_list` is O(n). If this sits inside a loop over m items, total cost is O(m × n). Convert the list to a set before the loop: `my_set = set(my_list)`, then test `x in my_set` for O(1) each.

common_mistakes.title: Inserting at the front of a list
common_mistakes.explanation: `my_list.insert(0, val)` is O(n) because every existing element must shift right. If you need fast prepends, use `collections.deque` which offers O(1) appendleft.

common_mistakes.title: Assuming dict/set are always O(1)
common_mistakes.explanation: O(1) is the average case. In pathological situations with many hash collisions, lookup degrades toward O(n). In practice this is extremely rare with built-in types, but custom __hash__ implementations that return a constant will trigger it.

common_mistakes.title: Sorting a list on every request
common_mistakes.explanation: list.sort() is O(n log n). If the data rarely changes, sort once and reuse the result, or maintain a sorted structure, instead of re-sorting on every API call.

recall_questions.q: What is the time complexity of checking `x in my_list` versus `x in my_set`, and why are they different?
recall_questions.answer: list membership is O(n) because Python must scan each element linearly. set membership is O(1) on average because sets use a hash table to jump directly to the right bucket.

recall_questions.q: Name two list operations that are O(1) and one that is O(n).
recall_questions.answer: Index access `xs[i]` and append are O(1). Insert or delete at position 0 is O(n) because every element must shift.

recall_questions.q: Why is choosing a set or dict for membership checks the single most impactful optimisation for collection code?
recall_questions.answer: Because membership testing drops from O(n) to O(1), and when that test is inside a loop the total cost drops from O(m × n) to O(m).

recall_questions.q: What is the average-case complexity of dict get, set, and delete operations?
recall_questions.answer: All three are O(1) on average because dicts use a hash table for direct key access.

recall_questions.q: Why does `x in my_tuple` have the same complexity as `x in my_list`?
recall_questions.answer: Tuples have no hash table — they are scanned linearly just like lists, so membership is O(n).

practice_tasks.title: Modify List to Set Lookup
practice_tasks.prompt: The provided function checks for allowed roles using a list, making it O(n) per check. Modify it to convert the `allowed` roles to a set first, so that the lookup inside the loop is O(1).

code_walkthrough.code:
import time

ids = list(range(1_000_000))
id_set = set(ids)
target = 999_999

t0 = time.perf_counter()
target in ids
list_ms = (time.perf_counter() - t0) * 1000

t0 = time.perf_counter()
target in id_set
set_ms = (time.perf_counter() - t0) * 1000

print(f'list: {list_ms:.4f} ms, set: {set_ms:.4f} ms')
code_walkthrough.focus: The set lookup finishes in microseconds while the list scan takes milliseconds — same data, same question, wildly different cost.

aha_moment.prediction: Both loops run 1000 iterations and neither finds -1. But which loop finishes noticeably slower?
aha_moment.common_guess: They take about the same time because neither finds the item
aha_moment.why: Not finding an item in a list is the worst case: Python must scan all 100 000 elements every iteration, making 100 million comparisons. The set checks a hash bucket and returns immediately each time, finishing almost instantly.

challenge.title: Build a fast request-dedup middleware cache
challenge.prompt: Write a class `RequestDedup` that stores recently seen request fingerprints (strings). It must support: `add(fingerprint)` to record a request, `is_duplicate(fingerprint) -> bool` to check in O(1), and `clear_older_than(cutoff: float)` that removes entries added before a Unix timestamp. Store each fingerprint with its timestamp. Choose data structures so that `add` and `is_duplicate` are O(1) average.

understanding_checks.question: A developer thinks that `x in my_list` and `x in my_tuple` have different time complexities because tuples are immutable. Why is this incorrect?
understanding_checks.answer: Both lists and tuples are linear sequences without hash tables. Therefore, membership testing (`in`) requires a linear scan (O(n)) for both.
understanding_checks.why: Immutability affects whether the structure can change, not how items are looked up. To achieve O(1) lookups, a hash table (like in a set or dict) is required.

understanding_checks.question: Why is inserting an element at the beginning of a list `my_list.insert(0, val)` considered an O(n) operation?
understanding_checks.answer: Because lists are contiguous arrays in memory. Inserting at the front requires Python to shift every subsequent element one position to the right.
understanding_checks.why: Understanding memory layouts helps predict performance costs. If frequent front-insertions are needed, `collections.deque` is a better choice.

understanding_checks.code:
data = [1, 2, 3]
my_set = set(data)
print(-1 in data == -1 in my_set)
understanding_checks.question: What does this code print?
understanding_checks.why: Both membership tests correctly evaluate to False (so False == False is True). However, the list scan is O(n) while the set check is O(1).
